did my job.

think those are probably some of them.

feel like it goes for a lot of us.

I really learned that I love roles that let me combine both content creation and that creative aspect in writing with sort of the behind-the-scenes analytical process thinking.

And my recent role, I've done a lot of AI content analytics where I've been sort of on the flip side where a lot of my work is actually going in to train the models in much more of a behind-the-scenes way than some like that more direct programming that you guys are working on with yours.
And it's been a really cool journey to, you know, 10 years ago when I was really getting started in my career, AI did not exist in the form it does now.
So it's been fun to see how my skills can evolve and like jump into those different aspects.

And yeah, really looking for the next stage in my career where I can take my various like project management skills and like the ownership of some of those big key pieces for clients and things and apply it in a much more meaningful way with organizations.
I'm kind of a small cog in the work that I do right now.
Which is fine, like, you know, we all are in those roles at some point in our lives.
And that's, you know, felt like a good place to start in terms of just getting into the AI landscape.

It's kind of high level, but.

And like, in the day to day of right now for you, like, what are some of the things that really energize you?
And then kind of the opposite of that, like, what really drains the life out of you?
Totally.
I'm a self-professed nerd, and I love problem solving.
So we also, we have a lot of nitty gritty tasks that we do.
Recently, we have been working on.
Do
We're sort of comparative analysis of different AI models where we're moving content from models into our software platform.
And we have to make sure that all the markdown transfers correctly.
Otherwise, you're not getting an accurate read of the response.
And I'm one of the people that goes through line by line and makes sure all that markdown is actually right.
And, you know, it can be really mind-numbing when you're looking at 10 of those responses in a couple of hours.
And so to sort of, like, help my brain not just totally flatline, I played around with some different approaches to, like, find and replace.
I dug into, like, regular expressions a little bit, which isn't something that I had played with a lot, just to, like, figure out a way to make it less painful.
And then once I really dialed that in, there were a couple of us who were kind of working on some different solutions.
We dialed that in.
And then the other piece I really love is, like, teaching other people how to do that, too.
Especially folks, like, you know, it's a fairly isolated.
When you're remote and you're working on these solo projects.
so there's a community of us that have sort of built up.
And so I've reached out to the leads and to some of my peers and offered to run little workshops on some of these solutions.
And that element of giving back to the folks that I work with is really important to me.

And so that was a huge piece for me in the last couple of weeks where I felt like I was contributing to more than just the project outcomes, but also to like everyone that I work with in the whole environment.
So, I mean, kind of hits on both ends, right?
Like the road of the day-to-day can be a little bit mind-numbing at times.
And yeah, that problem solving and that community building, I think, really helps me get through all of those aspects.

That makes sense.
like, how do you think about like the heads down work necessary versus like the interacting either with customers or, you know?
Or stakeholders or people that you need in order to get the right inputs, you know, to do a good job.
Yeah, you know, it's a balance, right?
Which sounds really wishy-washy.
I think a lot of it comes down to knowing your key timelines on important projects and like what's going to move the needle.

It's important to know when those conversations need to happen like immediately and have some urgency to them to get, you know, say you need specific information about like their project goals for like the tone or voice or you have, yeah, a bigger question that's, you know, stalling out the project.
Like prioritizing that conversation would totally make sense.
But there's also merit in prioritizing just building that relationship and building that trust, right?
Like there's, the trust comes not only from delivering really good products and in being consistent and reliable, but also having clients feel like they can come talk to you.
um, I was a supervisor at a ski resort for a couple of years with a kid's program and like parents want to know that you're going to show up and care for their kids, right?
Like not only.
Here are the lessons expensive, but they're handing you their most precious belonging in life, essentially.
And so being able to meet both of those needs, right?
Like getting kids out on the hill for a good lesson, but also reassuring nervous parents.
And I think that can translate even into the more technical aspects like this.
Yeah, yeah, that's cool.
And like what's kind of driving you right now?
And like if you think about like the next like one to two years, right?
Like what drives you professionally?
Yeah, similar to what we were talking about a little bit earlier, where I'm really hungry to learn new skills.
I've never been one to just kind of like sit around and wait for life to happen to me.
I have taught myself all sorts of like funky things in jobs.
When I was just out of college, I had no Excel experience.
And I discovered VLOOKUP formulas for a fundraising job that I was doing, like saved hours of work that was going to have to happen.
And then everywhere that
I've loved taking that with me and building on it.

And so in every opportunity, and especially this one going forward, it's really important to me to find things where I can bring my skills and feel like I can contribute pretty quickly, even if I have to learn on the fly and dig into something.
But that there's also a lot of opportunity for me to continue to build my relevant skills and new skills too.
I exploded new ideas, new concepts, new tools.
That's super important to me.
And so it's, you know, I want to advance my career and like, you know, have better titles and make more money and like all of those typical things that people want.
But I also really want to make sure that my own like skill building and learning and growth is happening alongside it.
Nice, nice.
And like where, like where in the last like few years you feel like you've learned or what phase you learned the most or, you know.
Yeah.
Whether it was a more technical skill or some of.
My own personal soft skills and my own confidence in my career and things.
I think that I have learned a lot about the ins and outs of content analytics in my current job.
Really fast-paced editorial work, getting through high-volume cases in a ton of time.
so really learning how to prioritize what good enough looks like in a lot of those cases and being able to burn through it quickly.
And knowing the most critical elements to look for in an editorial aspect.
But in terms of my personal growth as a mentor and manager and project management, I really gained a lot from the work that I did at that ski resort.
was there for a couple of years, first as an instructor and then a supervisor.

I was with difficult situations.
situations.
was I I was

In terms of like clients being really upset, you know, parents grumpy about the quality of lessons that their kids were getting from other instructors and having to be able to like take a breath and get them to take a breath and talk through things and problem solve.
And that was something that five years ago I would have felt quite nervous going into.

And so like, how would someone that either managed you directly or worked really closely with you describe you?
Really good problem solver.

That I was really good at getting projects across the finish line from the initial ideation as a group to the actual experience.
Execution.

So in my work right now, we, like I said, it's kind of an isolated job.
There's not always opportunity to give feedback and help mentor folks.

And coming in like as a baseline curator, there was a lot of times that I was kind of, you know, just starting to learn the ropes, wasn't totally sure all the ins and outs of what was supposed to be happening.
So I found folks to go ask questions to.

And as I moved through the ranks, I started, you know, gently advocating for processes that would help us give feedback to folks who had been in my position so that as a team, we could learn and grow.
And within all of that, it was really important to me to...
And feedback in a way that folks could hear.
We all have slightly different preferences in terms of the kind of feedback that we get and how it's received.

And I don't always hit the mark, but it's important to me that when I don't, that I can work with someone to understand how they would receive something better in the future, especially if I'm working closely with them consistently.
Um, and, and so there were a couple different instances where, you know, I, I might've just given too much information to overwhelm someone.
They're like, whoa, whoa, I did not need all of that.
Um, and so then moving forward, like we built a much stronger working relationship as, you know, I was able to kind of fine tune the exact kind of feedback that they, um, that they appreciated.
And so that, you know, that kind of approach, my, my leadership knew that that was something that was important to me.
Um, and I think they would, they would highlight that.

any kind of recent things as you're playing with like LLMs, um, that were like big unlocks for you that you learn or that playing around, like our little, little thing.
Things that you use more regularly and now?
Yeah.
One of the more recent, you know, in the last year, changes is this, like, deep research aspect of LLMs.
I did a lot on the back end of creating, like, rubrics and some of the chain of thought reasoning to train some of these models and how to make those jumps.
And I remember first using large language models when I was doing some, like, content curation for, like, a research-driven site.
And I kept asking it to, like, pull together, you know, really good research papers on certain topics, and it would just spit out make-believe papers at me, and I got so sick of it.

And so seeing that growth in these models' ability to actually, like, pull content and give you sources that you can go and, like, track down and make sure the information's actually accurate has been huge.
It's kind of changed the way I go about, especially even doing something really technical that needs a lot of, like, grounding and factuality.
I'll sort of kick off with one of those deep research queries, and it, you know, spits out a massive essay that's not necessarily the format that's useful.
But it kind of gets myself and also gets the model to think about the context that's going to be important to synthesize that into a much shorter report later.
Yeah, that makes sense.

It's funny, was doing research earlier today on an investment memo that I'm writing, and then it kept citing this one thing, and I kept asking it to, like, go find me only recent research and go find me the actual source, not an article.
And I kept mentioning this one source.
So I click on the source, and it's like the stat about, like, marketers being burned out, and a recent stat.
And then I click through, and it goes to a Forbes article.
And then I click through the Forbes article that mentions the same stat, and it goes to this other very not reputable page.
And then I click through that, and it's a link to a PDF from 2020 during COVID to help people were burned out.
And I was just like, how is this recent?
Like, first of all, why the  did you say recent on an article?
From five five years ago.
But also, like, for Flexity, it had, like, no ability to go chase the trail back to, like, the original source.
So, like, those are always, like, fun when you're, like, kind of doing that.
It's like, wait, hold on.
Before I put this in a citation for an investor to read and think I'm full of , like, let me just go double check this, you know?
Right, right.
I think it's, like, a lot of those things we're trying to solve for and where you need the human judgment of going recent, recent when, like, you know?
Yeah, and recent that it got cited, you know, was the Forbes article recent and that's what's getting pulled or was the actual research up to snuff?
Yeah, exactly.
And so, so that was always fun.

I think you answer, like, most of my questions.
been lovely, yeah.

I was curious, like, I've, you know, I've got some technical elements, but another sort of culture-based question.
Um, when...
When it comes to approaching failure or mistakes, what are some things that are important to you personally, and how do you hope that the team is going to approach those as well, right?
Like new territory, funky things are going to happen.
Obviously, quality of the end product is super important, but say something happens that creates big kerfuffle with the timeframe of a project.
Yeah, what are some ways that you guys approach that as a team and via management?
Yeah, I think it's like, if you can, I think there's two sides.
So like one is taking risk and trying new things to learn internally and seeing if it's good or not, versus like taking risk of like shipping garbage on a customer's website.

And then like that, that's like, I think those are different things like, and so there's a lot we can do internally to take a ton of risk and try different things and see what works and what doesn't work, right?
And I think that's very positive and all about like, what are we learning from it?
Hey, didn't work.
Let's keep, you know.
Let's try something else.
And like our speed of learning is like a competitive advantage we have, right?
Whereas like with customers, like we do want to take like very thoughtful and intentional bets and learn fast.
We just don't want to take, we just don't want to let, not have a filter for garbage, essentially.
Like then that once you break trust with customers, then it really takes a while to rebuild, right?
Like when they think they need to like look over a shoulder and every single word and every single thing, then it's like, oh shoot, like now it's going to take a while.
And sometimes it's unrecoverable because they're never going to think we're going to pay attention to details for them, right?

And so in some ways it's better to like say like delay things, but ideally it's like how do you build trust early on so that you can take more liberty and have more freedom to try new things and kind of trust your, like customers trust your thought process.
But with that being said, there's just so much here also to test and try that it's just fun.
And I think the way I used to do it early on, like last year when I was even more hands-on than I am right now on accounts, was just like every week I would come with some fun thing I did for them.
And it was just like, hey, okay, I was just doing this for fun.
I don't want us to use it, but I just thought, what if I rewrote some of our pieces in a movie character's voice just for fun?
And it was like, oh my God, this is so cool, blah, blah, blah, blah.
Or just be excited.

Or it's just like, hey, I did this other experiment where I just scraped 1,000 pages from your sitemap and came up with a scoring mechanism and which ones are like really not great and like, and why they're not and areas where they can be improved.
Like, oh my God, we've been wanting to do something like this, blah, blah, blah.
You know, so it just builds trust with them that you're like, it doesn't need to be every week.
But it's like, you're having some fun along way.
Yeah, that makes sense.
So you're like, you're showing one that you're like thinking, like we're talking about earlier, right?
Like thinking beyond the checkbox scope of the work.
And yeah, kind of, there's a vision of like throwing spaghetti at the wall, but in a very contained way.
So that the mess isn't impacting, yeah, the reputation of GrowthX or of your clients.
Because I can imagine that being really important.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, exactly.
Makes sense.
One other question.

I was curious, in terms of like the, you know, the rapid evolution of AI, how are you guys approaching as a team keeping folks' skills kind of up to speed as different like tools and products and things come through?
Like, do you, do you, you carve out time for like going to other workshops and tuning into like other, other AI tools and sources?
Do you, do you fit that in kind of?
Where you can, because I imagine there's a lot of tight deadlines and things.
What's your approach?
Yeah, yeah.
I don't think we've had like a great approach in the sense of like doing anything beyond the normal.
It's just that like as a company, we do a lot to educate people and try to give away the farm.
And I think we try to encourage everyone to consume that as well.

And so it's almost like instead of just writing docs and training and things, me internally and only sharing with our employees, like I tend to do workshops externally quite a bit and things like that.
So I think that helps.
I think the, but I don't think we structure the teams in a way that has allowed them enough time to just really go try to explore and learn.
Although some people do it on their own, you know, like, but then I think there's like a lot of things that we try to share.
Like, so our chief content officer, Matthew Panzarino, ran TechCrunch for 10 years, like he was
Like trying a bunch of different things with Cloud Projects.
And I did too.
And so it's like, hey, we should use Cloud Projects.
Here's how we're using it.
And we standardize it for the teams to use.

And I think creating even these assignments now for like part of the hiring process and things like that tries to just like get people to kind of explore in order to even be here, you know, and try new things in order to be here.
But I think like it's always tricky because there's like you can it can be just a full time job if people just play around every new thing.
Shiny new toy that's out there.
So sometimes like me and a couple of other folks are a little bit of the filter for that.
But yeah, welcome more ideas, you know, as well.
Yeah, totally.
Well, and I imagine a lot of it depends on, you know, what time folks happen to have like in between projects potentially.
And like you're talking about that intellectual curiosity and going like, oh, I want to go explore this thing.
And, you know.
Yeah, it's always easier when you have an output that you're trying to solve for.
Totally.
apply new things for that output.
